coredata.dc:title: Constructing highly efficient and ultra-stable (against Temperature, Humidity and Sunlight) luminescent solar concentrators by using dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xinchu
Wu, Jun
Huang, Danzhou
Yan, Jiaheng
Li, Changwen
Ren, Zhiqiang
Li, Jinhua
Wang, Xianbao
Wang, Jianying
coredata.dc:description: Conventional perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) suffer long-term stability, further limiting practical application of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). In this work, novel dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles with bright solid-state green light emission and 46 % of photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) have first been synthesized via one-step solid-phase calcination method. The dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have then been used as fluorescent material for construction of LSCs along with using organosilicon polymers with better compatibility as waveguide materials. Single-layer thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs are easily prepared by the casting and curing method, exhibiting high external optical efficiency (η opt) of 7.88 % and 10.92 %, respectively. The maximum internal quantum efficiency (η int) of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs reach 45.09 % and 44.07 %, respectively, while their maximum external quantum efficiency (η ext) of 29.42 % and 34.07 % are obtained, respectively. Importantly, both types of LSCs show excellent stability, maintaining above 92 % of the initial η opt under high-relative-humidity (RH = 90 %), high-temperature (60 °C) and sunlight exposure conditions for two weeks or room temperature environment for twenty weeks. Moreover, both types of LSCs at optimal conditions have good transparency (>40 % over 515 nm of light wavelength). Otherwise, both types of LSCs have higher η opt than that of the previous related work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dendritic particles
Perovskite quantum dots
LSCs
High stability
External optical efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles
Optical properties and performances of LSCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2023020201010139 ), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education, Hangzhou Normal University ( KFJJ2022008 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060 , 11574075 ), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020 ), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education, Jianghan University ( JDGD-202205 ), and the Ministry of Science, Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation ( D18025 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of preparation and configuration of LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles.

TEM images of (a) DMS and (b) dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. (c) HRTEM image of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. The inset is the lattice structure of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. (d) HAADF-STEM image of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. Elemental mapping images of (e) Cs, (f) Pb, (g) Br, (h) Si, and (i) O.

(a) XRD, (b) Full XPS spectra, (c) UV − vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. The inset is the photograph of the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (d) PL spectra of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles at various excitation wavelengths. (e) PL decay curve of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles under an excitation wavelength of 375 nm.

Photographs of (a) thin film LSCs and (b) overall curing LSCs with different dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations under daylight and 365 nm UV light, respectively. (c, d) Edge-emission photographs of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs under 365 nm UV light, respectively.
UV absorption, transmittance spectra and PL spectra of (a–c) thin film LSCs, (d–f) overall curing LSCs with different dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations, respectively.

Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J − V) of (a) thin film LSCs and (b) overall curing LSCs. The corresponding external optical efficiency histogram of (c) thin film LSCs and (d) overall curing LSCs.

Photographs of (a) thin film LSC based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles, (b) overall curing LSC based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles and (c) thin film LSC based on CsPbBr3 QDs under 365 nm UV light before and after 14 days treatment, respectively. (d) The corresponding normalized external optical efficiencies of LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles and CsPbBr3 QDs under the high-relative-humidity and high-temperature conditions (90 %RH, 60 °C) for different times, respectively. (e, f) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J-V) and (g, h) the corresponding external optical efficiencies of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles under room temperature environment for different times, respectively.

head.ce:title: Constructing highly efficient and ultra-stable (against Temperature, Humidity and Sunlight) luminescent solar concentrators by using dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education, Hangzhou Normal University, Hangzhou 311121, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) suffer long-term stability, further limiting practical application of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). In this work, novel dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles with bright solid-state green light emission and 46 % of photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) have first been synthesized via one-step solid-phase calcination method. The dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have then been used as fluorescent material for construction of LSCs along with using organosilicon polymers with better compatibility as waveguide materials. Single-layer thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs are easily prepared by the casting and curing method, exhibiting high external optical efficiency (η opt) of 7.88 % and 10.92 %, respectively. The maximum internal quantum efficiency (η int) of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs reach 45.09 % and 44.07 %, respectively, while their maximum external quantum efficiency (η ext) of 29.42 % and 34.07 % are obtained, respectively. Importantly, both types of LSCs show excellent stability, maintaining above 92 % of the initial η opt under high-relative-humidity (RH = 90 %), high-temperature (60 °C) and sunlight exposure conditions for two weeks or room temperature environment for twenty weeks. Moreover, both types of LSCs at optimal conditions have good transparency (>40 % over 515 nm of light wavelength). Otherwise, both types of LSCs have higher η opt than that of the previous related work.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are new type of light concentrators that can realize solar energy harvesting, transmission and conversion. LSCs have several benefits over conventional silicon-based solar cells and physical concentrators, including high light harvesting efficiency, easy preparation, low cost, high transparency, lightweight, simple color tunability, and the ability to operate in dispersed light [1,2]. Based on the above advantages, LSCs are very suitable for integrating with buildings and thus have great potential for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs), such as windows, glass curtain walls, etc [3]. In general, LSCs are composed of fluorescent materials, waveguide materials and edges-coupled solar cells. The fluorescent materials can be coated on the surface of the transparent waveguide substrate (such as transparent glass) to prepare thin film LSCs. The fluorescent materials also can be directly dispersed in the optical waveguide substrate to form overall curing LSCs. The photovoltaic cells are placed at the edges of the LSCs. LSC efficiency (e.g., external optical efficiency (η opt)) mainly depends on the development of fluorescent materials with high performances (e.g., high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY)). So far, various fluorescent materials, such as organic dyes [4–6], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecules [7,8], inorganic quantum dots (QDs)(e.g., CdS, InP) [9–13], carbon dots (CDs) [14,15], perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) [16,17], etc., have been used to prepare LSCs. However, some of luminescent materials are currently facing several challenges, such as the poor photostability and high reabsorption of dye molecules [18], the complex synthesis of AIE molecules [7], the relatively low PLQY of CDs in waveguide substrate [19], and the toxicity for inorganic quantum dots [1], limiting the development of LSCs.

Among the aforementioned fluorescent materials, PQDs are considered as excellent candidates for LSC application because of their remarkable optoelectronic properties, high PLQY, tunable emission wavelength and multiexciton effect [20–22]. To date, LSCs based on PQDs have been widely studied in last decade [23–26]. Stokes shift engineering, doping and tandem strategies have been developed and used to optimize the luminescence properties of PQDs and enhance LSC efficiency. First, the Stokes shift can be enlarged and PLQY can be enhanced by doping strategy, further reducing the reabsorption losses and improving LSC performances. For example, Zhao et al. synthesized mixed halide perovskite CsPb(BrxI1-x)3 nanocrystals with large Stokes shift and the prepared LSCs obtained an external optical efficiency (η opt) of 2 % [23]. To further enhance the performances of LSCs, rare-earth ions (Yb3+, Er3+, Ce3+, etc.) doped PQDs with high PLQY have been introduced into the LSCs. Yb-doped perovskite nanocrystals with 164 % of PLQY led to an external quantum efficiency (η ext) of close to 3.7 % for a LSC of 25 cm2 [27]. Meanwhile, transition metal ion Mn2+-doped perovskite nanocrystals are demonstrated to obtain a large Stokes shift with almost zero reabsorption loss and high PLQY [28,29]. For instance, Wei et al. prepared Mn-doped multiple quantum well (MQW) Br-based PQDs with a large Stokes shift and 100 % of PLQY. Based on this, large-area (40 × 40 cm2) liquid LSCs with an η int of 7 % and an η opt of 11 ± 1 % were achieved [30]. In addition, the efficiency improvement of single-layer LSCs is limited, tandem LSCs have been structurally designed to absorb solar radiation from different regions while better simulating sunlight emission, thus effectively improving the efficiency of LSCs. One tandem LSC of ∼100 cm2 had been prepared based on blue emissive CDs and green/red emissive PQDs, exhibiting an η opt of ∼3 % [31]. Our groups also developed the tandem LSCs with η opt of 3.76 % based on blue-white emissive CDs and green/red emitting PQDs [19]. Although LSC researches based on PQDs have made great progress, there still exists the long-term stability issue for PQDs-based LSCs, especially under harsh condition, e.g., high temperature, high relative humidity and intense sunlight, limiting the practical application of LSCs [32]. Therefore, it remains a challenge to develop PQDs based single-layer LSCs with high efficiency and ultra-stability.

In this work, to solve the above issues, novel green emitting dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles with 46 % of PLQY are first synthesized in-situ by a simple one-step solid-phase calcination method and using dendritic mesoporous silica (DMS) as a template. Compared to conventional quantum dots, the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles show more advantages, including facile synthesis, solvent-free preparation, high PLQY and ultra-high stability. Then, thin film LSCs (25 × 25 × 2 mm3) with different concentrations are constructed by coating dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles/silicone dendrimers onto glass substrates. The overall curing LSCs (25 × 25 × 2 mm3) with different concentrations are prepared by injecting dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles/PDMS into molds followed by thermal curing. Importantly, the maximum η opt of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs reach 7.88 % and 10.92 %, respectively. The η int of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs are up to 45.09 % and 44.07 %, respectively, while their η ext of 29.42 % and 34.07 % are achieved, respectively. Remarkably, whether exposed to high temperature, high humidity and sunlight exposure environment for 14 days or left at room temperature environment for 20 weeks, both types of LSCs remain above 92 % of the original η opt, showing ultra-stability. In addition, under optimal condition, the transmittance of both types of LSCs can maintain above 40 % over 515 nm of light wavelength. Otherwise, compared to the related published results, both types of LSCs have higher η opt (Table S1). Thus, the obtainment of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles by the solvents-free solid-phase calcination method will facilitate the large-scale preparation of LSCs, further posing great potential in practical application of LSCs.

Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99 %), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %) and sodium salicylate (NaSal, 99.5 %) were procured from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co.. Triethanolamine (TEA, ≥99 %), ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7 %) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 38 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co.. Ltd. Lead (II) bromide (PbBr2, 99.9 %), cesium bromide (CsBr, 99.5 %) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co.. Silicone dendrimers were synthesized based on our previous report [33]. Guangzhou Jianyi Chemicals Imp & Exp Co provided the Tyzor® 722 titanate chelate. All of the essential components and curing agents for PDMS came from Dow Corning 184 optical adhesives.

Dendritic mesoporous silica (DMS) was first synthesized by following the previous publication [34]. 0.25 mmol CsBr, 0.25 mmol PbBr2, and 0.2 g DMS were mixed in a mortar thoroughly. And then, the mixture was transferred to a muffle furnace, where it underwent calcination at 600 °C for 20 min at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. After allowing the particles to cool naturally, they were cleaned three times with deionized water and ethanol, and then the precipitates were collected after centrifugation for 5 min and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles were finally obtained by finely grinding the precipitates.

Thin film LSCs were prepared by using the casting and curing method, as shown in Fig. 1 . Different mass fraction of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles were first mixed with silicone dendrimers in a reagent bottle, agitated for 0.5 h, and then ultrasonically treated for 0.5 h. A certain amount of catalyst (Tyzor® 722) was added to the aforementioned mixture and stirred for 5 min. Then, 200 µL of the suspension was pipetted and dropped on a glass substrate (25 × 25 × 2 mm3), which was left to naturally flow until the glass substrate was completely covered by suspensions. And then, the suspension film was kept and cured completely for 24 h at room temperature to obtain thin film LSCs. The specific formulations are listed in Table S2.

Overall curing LSCs were prepared in a similar way to that of thin film LSCs. In general, different mass fractions of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles were added to a mixture containing PDMS Sylgard 184 and curing agent (mass ratio of 10:1). To achieve the homogenous suspensions, the mixture was suitably agitated, sonicated and vacuumed for 1 h to remove the bubbles inside. The above suspension was then put into a mold and allowed to cure for 2 h at 70 °C. The overall curing LSCs were then obtained. The specific formulas are contained in Table S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are used as fluorescent material for constructing thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs (Fig. 1). Firstly, DMS was successfully synthesized by using a mixture of cationic surfactant (CTAB) and anionic surfactant (sodium salicylate) as template agent and triethanolamine as catalyst [34]. Following that, dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles were synthesized by one-step solid-phase calcination method and utilizing DMS as a template and CsBr/PbBr2 as precursors. The SiO2 shell layer not only plays a key role in distributing and stabilizing the CsPbBr3 QDs inside, but shows good compatibility with waveguide matrix (e.g., silicon polymers). There are three reasons why chose SiO2 as shell layer. On one hand, the inert SiO2 shell layer is employed to protect the CsPbBr3 QDs inside from being degraded by external water and oxygen, thus enhancing the stability of CsPbBr3 QDs. On the other hand, the SiO2 shell layer may efficiently distribute CsPbBr3 QDs uniformly and prevent irreversible aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching (ACQ) [35–39]. Importantly, the third is that the SiO2 shell layer also addresses the compatibility issue of CsPbBr3 QDs with the waveguide materials and further improves the dispersion of CsPbBr3 QDs in the organosilicon polymers waveguide.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) is used to observe the morphology of DMS and dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. The initial DMS exhibits a dendritic mesoporous structure with excellent monodispersity, as demonstrated in Fig. 2 a. The three-dimensional large pores in DMS facilitate the high loading of precursors of CsPbBr3 QDs. The size of each DMS is approximately 150 nm. In the meantime, Fig. 2b shows that dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles can be obtained and a large number of CsPbBr3 QDs are confined in the SiO2 shell layer, indicating that the high concentration of CsPbBr3 QDs are successfully encapsulated. The transformation mechanism from DMS to CsPbBr3@SiO2 had been demonstrated by the previous report [40]. In detail, the CsPbBr3 precursor starts to melt (melting point (567 °C)) and sublimate into the pores of DMS at a high temperature of 600 °C, while the pore structure of DMS gradually collapses and finally CsPbBr3 was encapsulated. During the cooling down process, CsPbBr3 crystallizes in the confined space to generate CsPbBr3 QDs. It is noteworthy that the two or more DMS undergo a certain degree of melting, necking and branching process during calcination, finally forming dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles with an increased size (Fig. 2c). The average size of CsPbBr3 QDs is about 12.61 nm, as shown in Fig. S1. Otherwise, a distinct lattice stripe pattern can be seen in the corresponding high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (the inset of Fig. 2c), indicating that CsPbBr3 QDs have good crystallinity. Additionally, the interplanar distance of 0.30 nm corresponds to the (200) crystal planes of cubic CsPbBr3, demonstrating the successful formation of CsPbBr3 QDs inside the SiO2 shell layer [41]. The elements Cs, Pb and Br are consistently distributed throughout the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles, as is seen from the HAADF-STEM element mapping in Fig. 2d–i, further confirming that the CsPbBr3 QDs are successfully obtained inside the SiO2 shell layer.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of DMS and dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are recorded, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The peak at 22.7° is responsible for the amorphous SiO2. Additionally, the diffraction peaks of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are found at 15°, 21°, 26°, and 31°, corresponding to lattice planes (100), (110), (111), and (200), respectively, which match those of the cubic CsPbBr3 phase (PDF#54-0752). However, there are a few diffraction peaks that match the typical PDF card of PbBr(OH) in the XRD pattern, showing the presence of PbBr(OH). This might be the result of creation of stable PbBr(OH) during the water-washing procedure where PbBr2 remaining on the surface after calcination reacts with water [42,43].

To demonstrate the successful dense encapsulation of CsPbBr3 QDs by the SiO2 shell layer, the surface pore structures of DMS and dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are investigated using nitrogen adsorption isotherms. Fig. S2a–c show the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, specific surface areas and corresponding pore size distribution of the pristine DMS and dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles, respectively. Compared to that of the initial DMS, the surface area of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles decreases from 362.11 to 16.13 m2 g−1, which indicates that after calcination, the mesoporous structure collapses and the DMS is completely turned into dense SiO2 (Fig. S2b). In addition, after calcination, the original pores no longer exist, which is probably related to growth of CsPbBr3 QDs in the mesopores of DMS (Fig. S2c). It is noted that at 600 °C the pore structure of DMS would not collapse after calcination without CsPbBr3 precursors and the surface area of DMS is about 394.93 m2 g−1, which is similar to that of DMS before calcination, indicating the presence of CsPbBr3 precursors play a key role in formation of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles (Fig. S3).

The element compositions of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles exhibit Cs 3d, O 1s, Pb 4f, C 1s, Si 2p and Br 3d signals in Fig. 3b. As shown in Fig. S4a–c, the characteristic peaks of Cs, Pb and Br of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are all consistent with previous report [44]. The characteristic peaks of Cs 3d could be fitted to two typical peaks at 738.9 and 724.9 eV, which correspond to Cs 3d3/2 and Cs 3d5/2. The Pb 4f XPS spectrum at 143.6 and 138.8 eV belong to Pb 4f5/2 and Pb 4f7/2, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 69.1 and 68.2 eV represent Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2, respectively. The existence of SiO2 is further confirmed by Si 2p (103.3 eV) and O 1 s (532.6 eV) spectra (Fig. S4d,e) [45]. These findings indicate that CsPbBr3 QDs are successfully contained inside the SiO2 shell layer.

Fig. 3c shows the UV–vis absorption spectra, PL excitation and emission spectra of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. It can be noticed that dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have a maximum excitation wavelength of 286 nm and a fluorescence emission wavelength of 513 nm. From the inset image of Fig. 3c, the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles exhibit intense green light emission under 365 nm UV irradiation. The Stokes shift of the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles is about 102 nm from the absorption and emission spectra in Fig. 3c. In order to obtain high PL emission of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles, the effect of different reaction conditions on the fluorescence intensities of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles is investigated, including concentration of CsPbBr3 precursor and calcination temperature (Fig. S5). It is found that the highest fluorescence intensity is obtained when the molarity of CsPbBr3 precursor is 0.25 mmol and the calcination temperature is 600 °C. In addition, all PL emission peaks of the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are located at nearly 513 nm with little change under excitation with different wavelengths of light from 280 nm to 460 nm, indicating no excitation dependence (Fig. 3d). It is noted that the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CsPbBr3@SiO2 is about 21 nm at 300 nm excitation wavelength, which is slightly wider than that (18 nm) of pristine CsPbBr3 QDs (Fig. S6). Furthermore, Fig. 3e shows the PL decay curves of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles, which is fitted for the triple-exponential decay kinetics. An average lifetime of 4.36 ns under 375 nm excitation and a PLQY of 46 % are obtained, and the parameters are presented in Table S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and performances of LSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Photographs of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs based on different dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations under UV light and daylight are shown in Fig. 4 a and b. When the concentrations of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles are low, the words can be clearly seen on the bottom of LSCs under daylight. As the concentrations of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles increase, a change from transparent to translucent state is observed, and green photoluminescence gradually increases under UV light. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, it is apparent that the PL of the slab edges of both types of LSCs are brighter than that of the top surface through total internal reflection (TIR) effect, demonstrating an obvious and strong light waveguide effect. Organosilicon polymers are selected as waveguide materials. On one hand, dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles feature a Si-O-Si network structure on their surface that resembles the chemical structure of organosilicon polymer and both also have a similar refractive index, enabling good compatibility. On the other hand, due to the low viscosity and numerous nanocavities of silicone dendrimers [33], high loads can be easily achieved for thin film LSCs. PDMS has excellent toughness and bending properties, which have great advantages in the fabrication of flexible LSCs. It is worth noting that both thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs have uniform green emission, indicating that dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have good dispersion in organosilicon waveguides. The SEM and fluorescent images further prove that the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have good distribution at low particles concentration (e.g., 1 wt% for thin film LSCs and 0.1 wt% for overall curing LSCs). While there exists a certain degree of agglomeration of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles at high particles concentration (e.g., 10 wt% for thin film LSCs and 1 wt% for overall curing LSCs) (Figs. S7 and S8).

The absorption, transmittance and PL spectra of two types of LSCs at different dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations are tested to better illustrate their optical characteristics. As shown in Fig. 5 a and d, the two types of LSCs have similar absorption behavior, their absorption increases with the increased concentrations of luminescent materials. In detail, for thin film LSCs, the absorbance at 15 wt% particles concentration is about 5.5 times higher than that at 1 wt% particles concentration. While, for overall curing LSCs, the absorbance at 2 wt% particles concentration is about 12 times higher than that at 0.1 wt% particles concentration. The transmittance of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs with different dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations are shown in Fig. 5b and e. When dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations gradually increase from 1 wt% to 15 wt%, the transmittance of thin film LSCs at 600 nm is changed from 71 % to 17 %. For overall curing LSCs, the transmittance at 600 nm decrease from 92 % to 8 % as dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations increase from 0 wt% to 2.0 wt%. It is likely that the higher absorption and enhanced scattering induced by the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles at high concentrations lead to a decrease of transmission. It is worth noting that compared to that of Si-CDs based LSCs reported by our group [33], LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have higher transparency at the same luminescent materials concentrations, which is mainly due to the smaller size and better compatibility of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles compared to that of Si-CDs. Both types of LSCs exhibit similar PL emission peaks as the increased concentrations of luminescent materials (Fig. 5c and f), the emission peaks were positioned roughly at 513 nm. Both PL emission intensities are gradually enhanced with the increase of the luminescent material concentrations. Collectively, the higher absorption will lead to the lower transparency. There is an ideal concentration that provides high performance while maintaining good transparency of LSCs. After further optimization, it is evident that the transmittance of thin film LSCs can surpass 40 % over 515 nm of light wavelength when the concentration of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles is 5 wt%. Meanwhile, at a concentration of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles of 0.3 wt%, the transmittance in the entire visible range (400～700 nm) can be greater than 40 % for overall curing LSCs. Otherwise, distance-dependent PL spectrum have been conducted, as shown in Fig. S9. With the increase of distance (L), the PL of the LSC happens to red-shift (∼4 nm), showing small self-absorption.

The typical J-V curves of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs with various dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations have been recorded to better illustrate their photovoltaic performances, for instance, external optical efficiency (η opt). Fig. 6 a and b show the J-V curves of thin film LSCs with dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations varying from 1 to 15 wt% and overall curing LSCs with dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations changing from 0.1 to 2.0 wt%, respectively. The results demonstrate the same trend for all LSCs: when the concentrations of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles increase, the photovoltages change little while the photocurrent density dramatically increases. The η opt was calculated and plotted by using the photocurrent density data (Fig. 6c and d), and the specific calculation method is described in the Supporting Information. With the increase of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations from 1 to 15 wt%, the η opt of thin film LSCs are 2.58 %, 4.38 %, 5.58 %, 6.27 %, 6.78 %, 7.20 % and 7.88 %, respectively. For overall curing LSCs, the η opt of 1.46 %, 3.70 %, 5.89 %, 8.68 %, 9.46 %, 10.00 % and 10.92 % are obtained when the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations are increased from 0.1 to 2.0 wt%, respectively. Higher η opt can be obtained with the increased dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations, probably resulted from the more absorption and increased PL emission. The detailed photovoltaic parameters of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs based on various dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations are listed in Tables S5 and S6, including values for J sc, V oc, FF, G, C, η opt and PCE. Furthermore, both types of the LSCs show good repeatability since the η opt give the similar values after multiple measurements, as shown in Fig. S10. Otherwise, larger area (50 × 50 × 2 mm3) LSCs have been fabricated. The current density–voltage (J-V) curves and the corresponding external optical efficiencies have been provided in Fig. S11. The η opt of the larger thin film LSCs at 5 wt% of particles concentration and overall curing LSCs at 0.7 wt% of particles concentration are 1.51 % and 2.96 %, respectively. To further demonstrate the advantages of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles based LSCs, the spherical CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have been synthesized based on mesoporous silica template with uniform mesopores structure by using solvent-free calcination method [40], as shown in Fig. S12a and b. We assume that the mesoporous silica template with various mesopores structure plays a key role in the morphology of CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles and distribution of CsPbBr3 QDs in SiO2 particles. The mesoporous silica with uniform mesopores structure leads to spherical CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. Obviously, there exists a certain degree of agglomeration of CsPbBr3 QDs in spherical SiO2 particles, as shown in Fig. S12b. However, dendritic mesoporous silica (DMS) template leads to dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles and the CsPbBr3 QDs have good distribution in dendritic SiO2 particles, as shown in Fig. 2a, b and c. Furthermore, compared to that of spherical CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles based thin film LSCs, dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles based thin film LSCs have higher absorption and stronger PL emission, as shown in Fig. S12c and d. Thus, based on this, higher external optical efficiency (η opt) for dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles based thin film LSCs can be achieved at the same particles concentration (5 or 15 wt%), as shown in Fig. S12e and f, which are probably resulted from the well-distribution of CsPbBr3 QDs (stronger PL emission) and enhanced sunlight harvesting (higher absorption).

To further demonstrate the optical efficiency, the PLQY, the edge-emission efficiency(η edge), and the absorption efficiency (η abs) of LSCs are measured by using the integrating sphere, as shown in Figs. S13 and S14. The detailed data are shown in Tables S7 and S8. Two types of LSCs show similar trends. On one hand, as the concentrations of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles increase from 1 to 15 wt%, the PLQYs of thin film LSCs are 66.49 %, 85.78 %, 96.19 %, 93.74 %, 91.86 %, 90.07 % and 88.59 %, respectively. The results show that the best case for thin film LSCs exists at 5 wt% of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentration. For overall curing LSCs, the PLQYs are calculated as 70.20 %, 81.83 %, 91.78 %, 95.19 %, 86.59 % and 83.95 % when the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations are increased from 0.1 to 2.0 wt%, respectively. Similarly, the highest PLQY is present for overall curing LSCs at 0.7 wt% of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentration. On the other hand, the η edge of both types of LSCs decrease with the increase of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations, decreasing from 67.82 % to 26.33 % for thin film LSCs and from 62.77 % to 22.14 % for overall curing LSCs, respectively, which is attributed to the scattering effect of particles. While the η abs of both types of LSCs relatively increase with particles concentrations, the η abs of thin film LSCs increases from 37.08 % to 86.76 %, and the η abs of overall curing LSCs increases from 31.06 % to 92.61 %, coming from the increased particles concentrations.

Based on PLQY, η edge and η abs, the internal quantum efficiency (η int) and external quantum efficiency (η ext) are calculated, as shown in Tables S7 and S8. As dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles concentrations increase, the η int decreases from 45.09 % to 23.33 % for thin film LSCs, similarly, the η int of overall curing LSCs decreases from 44.07 % to 18.58 %. The η int has the same trend with η edge, probably resulted from the scattering effect of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. While the η ext first rises and then gradually falls. The highest η ext of 29.42 % and 34.07 % can be acquired for thin film LSCs at 5 wt% of particles concentration and overall curing LSCs at 0.7 wt% of particles concentration, respectively. The η ext shows the same trend with the PLQY, indicating the PLQYs play a key role in the performance enhancement of LSCs.

The stability of LSCs is first evaluated under a high-relative-humidity and high-temperature condition (90 %RH, 60 °C) (Fig. 7 a–d). Both types of LSCs are continuously kept in a 60 °C and water–vapor-enriched (90 %RH) oven. The J-V characteristic curves are measured periodically every 2 days. For better comparison, thin film LSCs based on colloidal CsPbBr3 QDs are prepared [46]. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, after treatment for 14 days, there has been no discernible difference in the PL emission of both types of LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles. While the PL emission of thin film LSCs based on colloidal CsPbBr3 QDs shows a decline (Fig. 7c), which may be due to degradation of colloidal CsPbBr3 QDs under high temperature and high humidity environment. Furthermore, Fig. 7d shows a change of the relative η opt under high temperature and high humidity environments, further demonstrating that the LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have ultra-high stability compared to that of the LSCs based on colloidal CsPbBr3 QDs. The η opt of both types of LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles remain above 95 % of the initial η opt, however, the η opt of thin film LSCs based on colloidal CsPbBr3 QDs drop below 80 % of original η opt after 6 days. The photostability has been conducted, as shown in Fig. S15, the optical efficiencies can remain above 92 % of the original η opt after two weeks of sunlight exposure. Thus, both the LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles show good photostability. In addition, to demonstrate the reliability of the LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles in long-term use, both types of LSCs are subjected to room temperature for 20 weeks. The J-V curves are tested, as shown in Fig. 7e and f, where the photocurrent densities of both types of LSCs only slightly decrease after 20 weeks. Fig. 7g and h further demonstrate that the LSCs based on dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles have ultra-high stability as their η opt still maintains above 95 % of the original η opt.

In summary, the dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles with bright solid-state green light emission are synthesized via one-step solid-phase calcination method and using monodisperse DMS as a template. Two types of single-layer LSCs including thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs are prepared by first using novel dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles as fluorescent material. Due to the high PLQY(46 %) of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles, the highest η opt of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs can reach 7.88 % and 10.92 %, respectively. The maximum η int of thin film LSCs and overall curing LSCs are up to 45.09 % and 44.07 %, respectively, while their maximum η ext of 29.42 % and 34.07 % are obtained, respectively. In addition, at optimal condition, the transmittance of both types of LSCs can maintain over 40 % above 515 nm of light wavelength. Although high optical efficiencies are achieved, the transmittance at high particles concentration is reduced greatly. Importantly, benefiting from the dense encapsulation of CsPbBr3 QDs by the SiO2 shell layer, both types of LSCs exhibit ultra-stable η opt under different harsh conditions. Otherwise, both types of LSCs have higher η opt than that of the previous related work (Table S1). Therefore, the creation of dendritic CsPbBr3@SiO2 particles is of great advantage for the development of highly efficient and ultra-stable LSCs.

Xinchu Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jun Wu: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Danzhou Huang: Formal analysis. Jiaheng Yan: Formal analysis. Changwen Li: Visualization. Zhiqiang Ren: Visualization. Jinhua Li: Supervision, Investigation. Xianbao Wang: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jianying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2023020201010139), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education, Hangzhou Normal University (KFJJ2022008), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060, 11574075), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education, Jianghan University (JDGD-202205), and the Ministry of Science, Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.148139.

ce:section.ce:para:
